source_metadata.title_raw: Gwen Kellas - A-19-155 - August 28, 2019 - San Simeon

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

August 28, 2019

Gwen Kellas
9231 Balboa Ave, Unit 4
San Simeon, CA 93452

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-19-155

Dear Ms. Kellas:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090.

Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice.

QUESTION

May you participate in decisions regarding the maintenance and repair, relocation, removal, and restoration of the San Simeon Community Service District's Wastewater Treatment Plant ("WWTP") site where you are chair of the District's Board of Directors and your condominium is within 500 feet of the WWTP site, and shares its bluff stability issues?

CONCLUSION

The facts indicate you have a reasonably foreseeable and material financial interest in decisions relating to the repair and maintenance, removal, and restoration of the WWTP site, as well as the adjacent riprap related Creek Plan decisions. This is due to your property's proximity and your unique interest in the WWTP site's stability issues. You may not take part in these decisions.

_______________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-19-155
Page No. 2

However, you may participate in decisions related to the relocation site(s), which the facts
indicate will be over 1,000 feet from your real property, to the extent that the relocation has been
required by the California Coastal Commission and the selection of the new location will not affect
decisions related to the existing WWTP site. You may also participate in the Overlook Plan
decisions, involving property within 500 to 1,000 feet, because it is not foreseeable from the facts
presented that the decisions will have a material effect on your property.

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

You serve as Chairperson of the Board of Directors, for the San Simeon Community Service
District ("District"), serving the community of San Simeon Acres. This is a small community with a
population of approximately 400 and covering approximately 100 acres. District Board members
are required to reside in the District area. The District services 250 connections, which includes
hotels and residential use. San Simeon Acres is bordered by the Hearst property, State Park property
and the ocean, and divided by Highway 1.

The District's Wastewater Treatment Plant ("WWTP") is located on a coastal bluff west of
Highway 1, and adjacent to Arroyo del Padre Creek. The WWTP is a "critical public
infrastructure." A gravity sewer system conveys San Simeon Acres domestic wastewater to the
WWTP. The WWTP bluff site is protected by a support wall built in the early 1960's, which has
been shored up with riprap over the years. The wall supports the entire end of the bluff.

The California Coastal Commission ("CCC") requires the District to relocate its WWTP
inland due to a risk of flooding and coastal contamination, as well as bluff erosion issues. The CCC
also requires the current WWTP site be restored to its natural habitat. You understand that this will
require the removal of the bluff support wall. The time frame for this project is ten years, with
possible extensions. You state that the future District Board decisions will likely center around the
WWTP issues.

Currently the District is searching for a new location, working to obtain grants for habitat
restoration, researching alternative solutions for waste treatment, considering a regional solution
with other stakeholders in the area (such as State Parks and Recreation), and exploring designs
related to these issues. The CCC Coastal Development Permit ("CDP") identifies the following
plans or items required of the District:

• A Coastal Hazards Response Plan. (Within three years) The District must
   ○ identify a preferred inland site or sites for District wastewater treatment functions
   ○ evaluate alternative wastewater treatment options in-lieu of building a new inland
     WWTP (including the construction of an inland package plant or plants, the
     possibility of combining services with other nearby existing WWTPs, and similar
     alternatives), with

• Repairs and Maintenance of Existing WWTP:
   ○ Additional Riprap Installation Plan; Riprap Revetment and Pipe Structure -
     Monitoring, Repair and Maintenance;

• Arroyo del Padre Juan Creek Enhancement Plan. (Within one year)

File No. A-19-155
Page No. 3

○ submit a plan for the removal of nonnative plant species and buried riprap along the
Creek area, and for the planting of native vegetation.

• Public Access Overlook Plan. (Within one year)
○ submit a plan to provide a public access overlook on State Parks' property (subject
to State Park's concurrence) on the blufftop overlooking the ocean near the
intersection of Balboa Avenue and Vista Del Mar Avenue, with bench, interpretive
sign and a connecting path back to the intersection.

• Plan and Dedication. (Within one year of cessation of WWTP operations)
○ submit a plan to decommission and remove the WWTP and all related development
○ restore the affected blufftop, bluff and creek areas to their natural conditions
(through re-contouring, removal of riprap, invasive plant removal, and planting of
native plants)
○ The Plan shall include removal of all WWTP components and infrastructure from
the WWTP site itself, and from any adjacent impacted properties.

You own and reside in a condominium located next to the WWTP, on Balboa Avenue, and
share the same bluff. The area of the Arroyo del Padre Creek slated for restoration and removal of
bluff-stabilizing riprap, is within 500 feet of your property. The Overlook is at the end of your street,
at the intersection with Vista Del Mar Avenue, located within 1,000 feet of your property. Decisions
related to the relocation of the WWTP are currently in the early stages. Counsel for the District
stated by telephone that two possible locations, one within the District boundaries and one outside
the District boundaries, were identified over ten years ago as potential relocation sites. Each site has
negative aspects, so other alternatives may be considered as well. It is known that the CCC will
require the relocation to site(s) that are away from the coastline, and thus will be east of Highway 1.
As a result, the relocation decision will likely involve property more than 1,000 feet from your
condominium. The other District Board members, as well as approximately 80 percent of the
residents in the District, reside on the inland side of Highway 1. Less than 20 % of the San Simeon
Acres residents reside on the west side, or coastal side, of Highway 1, where you reside.

You state that the WWTP retaining wall and riprap has secured the bluff, and you expect its
removal will lead to bluff erosion and a structural collapse of your condominium complex. You
believe that the construction disturbances will devalue your complex and that if you were to sell your
property, a notification of "massive future cost of the complex owners" would be required. As a
result, you believe that your condominium's market value is greatly impacted by the CCC's mandate
that the WWTP site be decommissioned and the site area restored. The CCC, in turn, has made this
mandate with the understanding that leaving the WWTP in its present location presents sewage
hazards to the community and erosion of the bluff as well. Your question is whether you may
participate in the District's WWTP decisions.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official has a financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental
decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a

File No. A-19-155
Page No. 4

material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on one or more of
the public official's interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).) The financial interest you
identified at issue is your real property interest in your condominium residence. (Section 87103(b))

Foreseeability and Materiality Standards

A financial effect is presumed reasonably foreseeable where the official's financial interest
is explicitly involved as a named party in, or subject of, the decision. (Regulation 18701(a).) A
financial interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision affects a real property interest
described in Regulation 18702.2(a)(1)-(6).² For a financial interest that is not explicitly involved in
the decision, such as described here, the financial effect is reasonably foreseeable if it can be
recognized as a realistic possibility and is more than a hypothetical or theoretical possibility.
(Regulation 18701(b).)

Real Property Interest: Materiality under Regulation 18702.2

As to materiality, where a government decision involves property located within 500 feet,
there is a presumption that the decision will have a material financial effect on the official's real
property interest, unless clear and convincing evidence is provided to the contrary. (Regulation
18702.2(a)(7).) Therefore, the District decisions related to repairing,³ removing or restoring the
WTP site located adjacent to your property will have a reasonably foreseeable and material
financial effect on your condominium. The Creek restoration plan, to the extent it involves the
removal of structural riprap that shores up the bluff, will have the same material financial effect.
You have a prohibited financial interest in these decisions, absent the provision of clear and
convincing evidence to the contrary.

The Public Access Overlook is located between 500 feet and 1,000 feet from your property.
For this range, the financial effect is material if the decision would change the parcel's development

² The current facts do not indicate the involvement of your property in items (1) through (6); although the
Plans submitted to the CCC may implicate several of these items, if, as you assert, the Plans will alter the stability of the
bluff, and thus alter your entitlements or use of your property. Regulation 18702.2(a), items (1) through (6) state:
(1) Involves the adoption of or amendment to a development plan or criteria applying to
the parcel;
(2) Determines the parcel's zoning or rezoning, other than a zoning decision applicable to
all properties designated in that category; annexation or de-annexation; inclusion or exclusion
from any city, county, district, or local government subdivision or other boundaries, other than
elective district boundaries;
(3) Would impose, repeal, or modify any taxes, fees, or assessments that apply to the
parcel;
(4) Authorizes the sale, purchase, or lease of the parcel;
(5) Involves the issuance, denial or revocation of a license, permit or other land use
entitlement authorizing a specific use of or improvement to the parcel or any variance that
changes the permitted use of, or restrictions placed on, the property;
(6) Involves construction of, or improvements to, streets, water, sewer, storm drainage or
similar facilities, and the parcel will receive new or improved services that provide a benefit or
detriment disproportionate to other properties receiving the services;

³ We note that Regulation 18702.2(d) provides an exception for decisions that solely concern repairs,
replacement or maintenance of existing water or sewer facilities. This exception is not applicable to
where the stability of
the bluff supporting your residence is impacted by the repair and maintenance decisions.

File No. A-19-155
Page No. 5

or income producing potential, change the property's highest and best use, market value, or the
property's character (by substantially altering traffic levels, intensity of use, parking, or view.)
(Regulation 18702.2(a)(8).) You indicate that the Overlook Plan mandated by the CCC involves the
addition of a bench, a path, and an interpretive sign for an area that is already accessible as an
"overlook." You do not anticipate any changes to your property's character, such as additional
traffic or intensity of use, related to these decisions. Therefore, based on these facts, you do not
have a financial interest in this type of decision.

Decisions related to the relocation of the WWTP will likely involve property more than
1,000 feet from your condominium and are thus presumed not to have a material financial effect on
your real property interest. (Regulation 18702.2(b).) This presumption may be rebutted if clear and
convincing evidence indicates the decision would have a substantial effect on your property. So
long as the relocation site(s) considered are located 1,000 or more from your residence, and there
are no indications that the decision would have a substantial effect on your property, you do not
have a financial interest in the WWTP relocation decisions. However, we caution that this
conclusion rests on the fact that the CCC has required the relocation. Accordingly, there is no
indication that the selection of a new location would affect decisions related to the existing WWTP
site.

Public Generally

An official does not have a financial conflict of interest where the financial effect is
indistinguishable from the financial effect that will be shared with the public. (Section 87103.) For a
financial effect of a decision to be "indistinguishable," Regulation 18703 requires that the financial
impact be shared by at least 25 percent of the public, and that the official's financial effect is not
unique. Here, the decisions related to the repair, removal or restoration of the WWTP site and the
removal of the riprap in the Creek area will have a unique impact on your condominium due to its
close proximity and reliance on the same bluff for stability. Few members of the San Simeon Acres'
public share your proximity and bluff-stability issues related to the WWTP. Therefore, your real
property financial interest is unique as to decisions that implicate the bluff's stability and the
standard in Regulation 18703 is not met.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

By: L. Karen Harrison
Counsel, Legal Division

LKH:aja

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or 
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the 
official has a financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental 
decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a  
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on one or more· of 
the public official's interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).) The financial interest you 
identified at issue is your real property interest in your condominium residence. (Section 87103 (b)) 
Foreseeability and Materiality Standards 
A financial effect is presumed reasonably foreseeable where the official's financial interest 
is explicitly involved as a named party in, or subject of, the decision. (Regulation l 8701(a).) A 
financial interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision affects a real property interest 
described in Regulation 18702.2(a)(l)-(6).2 For a financial interest that"is not explicitly involved in 
the decision, such as described here, the financial effect is reasonably foreseeable if it can be 
recognized as a realistic possibility and is more than a hypothetical or theoretical possibility.

As to materiality, where a government decision involves property located within 500 feet, 
there is a presumption that the decision will have a material financial effect on the official's real 
property interest, unless clear and convincing evidence is provided to the contrary. (Regulation

WWTP site located adjacent to your property will have a reasonably foreseeable and material

You have a prohibited financial interest in these decisions, absent the provision of clear and

For this range, the financial effect is material if the decision would change the parcel's development

bluff, and thus alter your entitlements or use of your property. Regulation 18702.2(a), items (I) through (6) state:

from any city, county, district, or local government subdivision or other boundaries, other than

(3) Would impose, repeal, or modify any taxes, fees, or assessments that apply to the

(4) Authorizes the sale, purchase, or lease of the parcel; 
(5) Involves the issuance, denial or revocation of a license, permit or other land use

and restoration of the San Simeon Community Service District's Wastewater Treatment Plant 
("WWTP") site where you are chair of the District's Board of Directors and your condominium is 
within 500 feet of the WWTP site, and shares its bluff stability issues?

CONCLUSION: The facts indicate you have a reasonably foreseeable and material financial interest in 
decisions relating to the repair and maintenance, removal, and restoration of the WWTP site, as 
well as the adjacent riprap related Creek Plan decisions. This is due to your property's proximity 
and your unique interest in the WWTP site's stability issues. You may not take part in these 
decisions.However, you may participate in decisions related to the relocation site(s), which the facts

presented that the decisions will have a material effect on your property.
